en.extract:
The little spotted kiwi (Apteryx owenii), also known as little grey kiwi or kiwi pukupuku, is a small flightless bird in the kiwi family, Apterygidae. It is the smallest of the five kiwi species, at about 0.9 to 1.9 kg (2–4+1⁄4 lb), about the size of a bantam. It is endemic to New Zealand, and in pre-European times occurred in both main islands, but is now mainly restricted to a number of small offshore islands and mainland reserves protected by pest-exclusion fences. 
The little spotted kiwi was on the brink of extinction when a conservation effort took place 100 years ago. Five individuals were translocated from the South Island to Kapiti Island. Today, the Kapiti Island population has grown, with around 1200 birds. They were considered extinct on the mainland, with no sightings since 1978, until their rediscovery in the wild in 2025.


Taxonomy
The little spotted kiwi is a ratite and belongs to the Apterygiformes order, and the Apterygidae family. The genus name Apteryx means 'without wings' and the species is named owenii after Sir Richard Owen. Only the nominate subspecies A. o. owenii survives.
The little spotted kiwi was first described in 1847 by John Gould from a specimen obtained by Frederick Strange and sent to England. The locality is not recorded but it probably came from Nelson or Marlborough. In 1873, Henry Potts published an account of its habitats, and about this time specimens were collected in South Westland and sent to England. A specimen described as Apteryx occidentalis in 1893 is often considered a junior synonym of this species, but a study in 2002 indicated this may be a hybrid between Apteryx australis and Apteryx owenii.
These is a subspecies called Apteryx owenii iredalei, from the North Island, but its status as a subspecies is not universally accepted as valid. More commonly known as the North Island little spotted kiwi, it is an extinct subspecies of the little spotted kiwi that was native to the North Island, New Zealand. It was described by Gregory Mathews in 1935. Most subsequent authors do not recognise it or any other subspecies of Apteryx owenii. The North Island little spotted kiwi went extinct sometime in the late 19th century for unknown reasons; most likely from introduced invasive species such as stoats, feral cats, and other related species, or from human activity in the region. Two specimens were collected in the 1880s, being the only known remnants left.


Description
The little spotted kiwi has a length of 35 to 45 cm (14–18 in) and the weight of the male is 0.88 to 1.36 kg (1 lb 15 oz – 3 lb 0 oz) and the female weighs 1 to 1.95 kg (2+1⁄4–4+1⁄4 lb), making it the smallest species of kiwi. Their feathers are pale-mottled grey, with fine white mottling, and are shaggy looking. They lack aftershafts and barbules. They have large vibrissae feathers around the gape. They lack a tail, but have a small pygostyle. Their bill is ivory-coloured and long and their legs are pale.

The little spotted kiwi had an obligate louse that parasitised on it, Rallicola pilgrimi. In an example of conservation-induced extinction, all lice were killed when the kiwi were translocated to Kapiti Island.


Distribution and habitat
Studies on Kapiti Island show that they prefer flax, seral, and older forest habitats. Lower numbers are found in rough grassland and scrub, indicating that either they prefer other habitats or they simply need a larger territory to support themselves in these areas.


Behaviour
Little spotted kiwis eat grubs and other small insects that are found underground, and occasionally eat berries. Earthworms, cockchafer beetle larvae, caterpillars, cranefly larvae, and spiders are their most common foods, with annelids making up the largest percentage of the bird's diet; fruit of the hinau tree is also commonly consumed. 
As they cannot fly and their eyesight is very poor, they depend on a keen sense of smell, long beak and talons for finding food. The birds use their sharp talons and long beak to dig into the ground and then probe its long beak through the softened ground. 
Little spotted kiwi are nocturnal, and will call occasionally each night to advertise its territory and maintain contact with partners; often pairs will duet. They are very territorial, and fight conspecifics with their sharp claws, resulting in many feathers on the ground.


Reproduction

They nest in an excavated burrow, dug by both birds and sometimes lined with plant material. Eggs are laid from July to January. The clutch size is one to two eggs (15% have 2), which are incubated by the male for 63–76 days. Chicks stay in the nest for 2–3 weeks after hatching and require feeding for 4 weeks.
Little spotted kiwi eggs weigh about 23% the weight of the parent bird—the largest ratio among kiwis and birds in general—but exceeded by some sea-living birds, such as the Guadalupe murrelet and storm petrel.


Conservation
At the time it was described (in the 1840s), the species was common on the western side of the South Island and in Marlborough. A regular trade in skins sprang up and large numbers were collected for European museums. Further, with the advance of European settlement, birds were killed by prospectors and others for food, and their attendant dogs and cats took their toll.
The species was extinct on the North Island by 1938 when the last four South Island birds were moved from d'Urville Island to the population that had been established on Kapiti Island. After they were released on Kapiti Island, they were also moved to Red Mercury Island, Hen Island, Tiritiri Matangi Island, Chalky Island, and Long Island in the Queen Charlotte Sound.
In 2000, about 20 little spotted kiwis were released into Zealandia. This was the first time since the 19th century that little spotted kiwis could be found on the mainland of the North Island. In 2015, 20 kiwis were translocated from Kapiti Island to Anchor Island.
As the smallest species of kiwi, they would be very vulnerable to the main kiwi predators like cats, dogs, and stoats, if not mostly restricted to several off-shore island reserves (mainly Kapiti Island) which are mostly free of introduced predators. The little spotted kiwi's conservation status is listed as "range restricted" by Save the Kiwi, with a growing population. Formerly classified as "vulnerable" by the IUCN, it was suspected to be more numerous than generally assumed. Following the evaluation of its population size, this was found to be correct, and it was consequently downlisted to "near threatened" status in 2008 as, although not rare, its small range puts it at risk.  The lack of predators, apart from weka, is important to its increasing numbers. It has an occurrence range of 31 km2 (12 sq mi), and a population of 1600 was estimated in 2012.
In May 2025, 40 birds (20 males and 20 females) were translocated from Kapiti Island to the Brook Waimārama Sanctuary in Nelson, the largest fenced sanctuary in the South Island. The last known population to have lived in the region disappeared nearly a century prior.
In July, two little spotted kiwi were rediscovered on the mainland (outside of a fenced sanctuary) for the first time since 1978, following a tip-off from a hunter. A female and male bird were found in the Adams Wilderness Area in the South Island's West Coast Region.


Citations


General and cited references

BirdLife International (2008b). "What's New (2008)". IUCN RedList. Archived from the original on 28 August 2007. Retrieved 4 February 2009.
Davies, S.J.J.F. (2003). "Kiwis". In Hutchins, Michael (ed.). Grzimek's Animal Life Encyclopedia. Vol. 8 Birds I: Tinamous and Ratites to Hoatzins (2nd ed.). Farmington Hills, MI: Gale Group. pp. 89–90, 92–93. ISBN 0-7876-5784-0.
Gotch, A.F. (1995) [1979]. "Kiwis". Latin Names Explained. A Guide to the Scientific Classifications of Reptiles, Birds & Mammals. London: Facts on File. p. 181. ISBN 0-8160-3377-3.
"Little spotted kiwi". Save the Kiwi. Retrieved 20 May 2023.


External links

BirdLife International (2023). "Species factsheet: Apteryx owenii". Data Zone. Retrieved 27 November 2023.

The '''little spotted kiwi''' ('''''Apteryx owenii'''''), also known as '''little grey kiwi'''<ref name="Davies" /> or '''''kiwi pukupuku''''', is a small flightless bird in the [[Kiwi (bird)|kiwi]] family, Apterygidae. It is the smallest of the five kiwi species, at about {{convert|0.9|to(-)|1.9|kg|lb|frac=4|abbr=on}}, about the size of a [[bantam (chicken)|bantam]]. It is endemic to New Zealand, and in pre-European times occurred in both main islands, but is now mainly restricted to a number of small offshore islands and mainland reserves protected by [[pest-exclusion fence]]s. 

The little spotted kiwi was on the brink of extinction when a conservation effort took place 100 years ago. Five individuals were translocated from the [[South Island]] to [[Kapiti Island]]. Today, the Kapiti Island population has grown, with around 1200 birds.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ramstad |title=Genetic consequences of a century of protection: serial founder events and survival of the little spotted kiwi (Apteryx owenii) |journal=Proceedings. Biological Sciences |date=2013 |volume=280 |issue=1762 |publisher=PubMed Central |doi=10.1098/rspb.2013.0576 |pmid=23677342 |pmc=3673049 }}</ref> They were considered extinct on the mainland, with no sightings since 1978, until their rediscovery in the wild in 2025.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Davies |first=Lisa |date=16 July 2025 |title=Endangered little spotted kiwi seen on mainland for first time in decades |url=https://www.1news.co.nz/2025/07/16/endangered-little-spotted-kiwi-seen-on-mainland-for-first-time-in-decades/ |access-date=2025-07-18 |website=1News |language=en-nz}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The little spotted kiwi is a [[ratite]] and belongs to the Apterygiformes order, and the Apterygidae family. The genus name ''Apteryx'' means 'without wings' and the species is named ''owenii'' after [[Sir Richard Owen]].<ref>Gotch, A. F. (1995)</ref> Only the [[nominate subspecies]] ''A. o. owenii'' survives.

The little spotted kiwi was first described in 1847 by [[John Gould]] from a specimen obtained by [[Frederick Strange]] and sent to England.<ref name="Whittell1947">{{cite journal |last1=Whittell |first1=H. M. |title=Frederick Strange |journal=The Australian Zoologist |date=1947 |volume=11 |pages=96–114 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/39059740#page/132/mode/1up}}</ref> The locality is not recorded but it probably came from Nelson or Marlborough. In 1873, Henry Potts published an account of its habitats, and about this time specimens were collected in South Westland and sent to England.<ref name = "Davies"/> A specimen described as ''Apteryx occidentalis'' in 1893 is often considered a [[junior synonym]] of this species, but a study in 2002 indicated this may be a [[hybrid (biology)|hybrid]] between ''[[Apteryx australis]]'' and ''Apteryx owenii''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Herbert |first1=John |last2=Daugherty |first2=Charles H. |title=Genetic variation, systematics and management of kiwi (''Apteryx'' spp.) |journal=Some Early 1990s Studies in Kiwi |date=2002 |url=https://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/document?repid=rep1&type=pdf&doi=8749d134b92b7063791b57637ef54203456cdfce |access-date=2 December 2024}}</ref>

These is a subspecies called ''Apteryx owenii iredalei'', from the [[North Island]], but its status as a subspecies is not universally accepted as valid.<ref name="hbw">{{cite journal |last1=Folch|first1=A. |last2=Jutglar|first2=F.|last3=Garcia|first3=E.F.J.|editor1-last=del Hoyo |editor1-first=Josep |editor2-last=Elliott |editor2-first=Andrew |editor3-last=Sargatal |editor3-first=Jordi |editor4-last=Christie |editor4-first=David A. |editor5-last=de Juana |editor5-first=Eduardo |year=2018 |title=Little Spotted Kiwi (''Apteryx owenii'') |journal=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive |url=https://www.hbw.com/species/little-spotted-kiwi-apteryx-owenii |publisher=Lynx Edicions |location=Barcelona, Spain |doi=10.2173/bow.liskiw1.01 |s2cid=243646391 |access-date=18 August 2018 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> More commonly known as the North Island little spotted kiwi, it is an [[Extinction|extinct]] [[subspecies]] of the little spotted kiwi that was [[Native species|native]] to the [[North Island]], [[New Zealand]].<ref name="savethekiwi">{{Cite web |title=Little Spotted kiwi |url=https://savethekiwi.nz/about-kiwi/kiwi-species/little-spotted-kiwi/ |access-date=19 May 2023 |website=Save the Kiwi |language=en-NZ}}</ref> It was described by [[Gregory Mathews]] in 1935. Most subsequent authors do not recognise it or any other subspecies of ''Apteryx owenii''.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Herbert |first1=John |url= |title=Some Early 1990s Studies in Kiwi |last2=Daugherty |first2=Charles H. |date=2002 |publisher=New Zealand Department of Conservation |isbn=0-478-22194-0 |editor-last=Overmars |editor-first=Fred |location= |page=31 |chapter=Genetic variation, systematics and management of kiwi (''Apteryx'' spp.) |access-date=20 May 2023 |chapter-url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/documents/science-and-technical/ir191a.pdf}}</ref> The North Island little spotted kiwi went extinct sometime in the late 19th century<ref name="savethekiwi" /><ref name="Extinct Birds2">{{cite book |last1=Hume |first1=J. P. |title=Extinct Birds |last2=Walters |first2=M. |publisher=A & C Black |year=2012 |isbn=978-1-4081-5725-1 |location=London |pages=23–24}}</ref> for unknown reasons; most likely from introduced [[invasive species]] such as [[Stoat|stoats]], [[Feral cat|feral cats]], and other related species, or from human activity in the region. Two specimens were collected in the 1880s, being the only known remnants left.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jolly |first1=J.N. |last2=Colbourne |first2=R.M. |date=June 1991 |title=Translocations of the little spotted kiwi (''Apteryx owenii'') between offshore islands of New Zealand |journal=Journal of the Royal Society of New Zealand |language=en |volume=21 |issue=2 |pages=143–149 |bibcode=1991JRSNZ..21..143J |doi=10.1080/03036758.1991.10431402 |issn=0303-6758 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

==Description==
The little spotted kiwi has a length of {{convert|35|to(-)|45|cm|in|abbr=on}} and the weight of the male is {{convert|0.88|to(-)|1.36|kg|lboz|abbr=on}} and the female weighs {{convert|1|to(-)|1.95|kg|lb|frac=4|abbr=on}}, making it the smallest species of kiwi.<ref name=hbw/> Their feathers are pale-mottled grey, with fine white [[mottling]], and are shaggy looking.<ref name="BLI2">BirdLife International (2008a)</ref>{{full citation needed|date=August 2019}} They lack [[wikt:aftershaft|aftershaft]]s and [[barbule]]s. They have large [[vibrissae]] feathers around the [[gape]]. They lack a tail, but have a small [[pygostyle]].<ref name="Davies" /> Their bill is [[Ivory (color)|ivory]]-coloured and long and their legs are pale.<ref name="BLI2"/>

<gallery widths="200px" heights="200px">
File: Apteryx owenii 0.jpg|Illustration by [[J. G. Keulemans]] from a work by [[George Dawson Rowley|G. D. Rowley]], 1870s
File:Kiwi burrow entrance.jpg|Burrow entrance, Zealandia
</gallery>The little spotted kiwi had an [[Obligate parasite|obligate louse]] that parasitised on it, [[Rallicola|''Rallicola pilgrimi'']]. In an example of [[conservation-induced extinction]], all lice were killed when the kiwi were translocated to Kapiti Island.<ref name="Rózsa2014">{{cite journal |last1=Rózsa |first1=Lajos |last2=Vas |first2=Zoltán |date=2014-08-22 |title=Co-extinct and critically co-endangered species of parasitic lice, and conservation-induced extinction: should lice be reintroduced to their hosts? |url=https://real.mtak.hu/17477/1/oryx.pdf |journal=Oryx |volume=49 |issue=1 |pages=107–110 |doi=10.1017/s0030605313000628 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=''Rallicola (Aptericola) pilgrimi''. NZTCS |url=https://nztcs.org.nz/assessments/20631 |access-date=2023-08-23 |website=nztcs.org.nz}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
Studies on [[Kapiti Island]] show that they prefer flax, [[Seral community|seral]], and older forest habitats. Lower numbers are found in rough grassland and scrub, indicating that either they prefer other habitats or they simply need a larger territory to support themselves in these areas.<ref name="BLI2"/>

==Behaviour==
Little spotted kiwis eat grubs and other small insects that are found underground, and occasionally eat berries. Earthworms, [[cockchafer]] beetle larvae, caterpillars, [[cranefly]] larvae, and spiders are their most common foods,<ref name=animaldiversity>{{cite web | url= https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Apteryx_owenii/ | title=Apteryx owenii (Little spotted kiwi) | website=[[Animal Diversity Web]] }}</ref> with [[annelid]]s making up the largest percentage of the bird's diet;<ref name="animaldiversity" /> fruit of the [[Elaeocarpus dentatus|hinau tree]] is also commonly consumed.<ref name=animaldiversity/> 

As they cannot fly and their eyesight is very poor, they depend on a keen sense of smell, long beak and talons for finding food. The birds use their sharp talons and long beak to dig into the ground and then probe its long beak through the softened ground.<ref name="Davies" /> 

Little spotted kiwi are nocturnal, and will call occasionally each night to advertise its territory and maintain contact with partners; often pairs will duet. They are very territorial, and fight [[conspecific]]s with their sharp claws, resulting in many feathers on the ground.

===Reproduction===
[[File:Little-spotted kiwi egg.jpg|thumb|Egg in hand]]
They nest in an excavated burrow, dug by both birds and sometimes lined with plant material. Eggs are laid from July to January. The clutch size is one to two eggs (15% have 2), which are incubated by the male for 63–76 days. Chicks stay in the nest for 2–3 weeks after hatching and require feeding for 4 weeks.<ref name="Davies" />

Little spotted kiwi eggs weigh about 23% the weight of the parent bird—the largest ratio among kiwis and birds in general—but exceeded by some sea-living birds, such as the [[Guadalupe murrelet]] and [[storm petrel]].{{Citation needed|date=July 2025}}

==Conservation==
At the time it was described (in the 1840s), the species was common on the western side of the South Island and in [[Marlborough District|Marlborough]]. A regular trade in skins sprang up and large numbers were collected for European museums. Further, with the advance of European settlement, birds were killed by prospectors and others for food, and their attendant dogs and cats took their toll.{{fact|date=October 2025}}

The species was extinct on the North Island by 1938 when the last four South Island birds were moved from [[d'Urville Island (New Zealand)|d'Urville Island]] to the population that had been established on Kapiti Island.<ref name="hbw" /> After they were released on Kapiti Island, they were also moved to [[Red Mercury Island]], [[Hen and Chicken Islands|Hen Island]], [[Tiritiri Matangi Island]], [[Chalky Island (New Zealand)|Chalky Island]], and [[Long Island, Marlborough|Long Island]] in the [[Queen Charlotte Sound (New Zealand)|Queen Charlotte Sound]].{{fact|date=October 2025}}

In 2000, about 20 little spotted kiwis were released into [[Karori Wildlife Sanctuary|Zealandia]]. This was the first time since the 19th century that little spotted kiwis could be found on the mainland of the North Island.<ref name="BLI2" /> In 2015, 20 kiwis were translocated from Kapiti Island to [[Anchor Island]].{{fact|date=October 2025}}

As the smallest species of kiwi, they would be very vulnerable to the main kiwi predators like cats, dogs, and [[stoats in New Zealand|stoat]]s, if not mostly restricted to several off-shore island reserves (mainly Kapiti Island) which are mostly free of introduced predators. The little spotted kiwi's conservation status is listed as "range restricted" by [[Save the Kiwi]], with a growing population. Formerly classified as "[[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]]" by the [[IUCN]],<ref>BirdLife International (2008b)</ref> it was suspected to be more numerous than generally assumed. Following the evaluation of its population size, this was found to be correct, and it was consequently downlisted to "[[near threatened]]" status in 2008 as, although not rare, its small range puts it at risk.  The lack of predators, apart from [[weka]], is important to its increasing numbers. It has an occurrence range of {{convert|31|km2|sqmi|frac=4|abbr=on}}, and a population of 1600 was estimated in 2012.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

In May 2025, 40 birds (20 males and 20 females) were translocated from Kapiti Island to the [[Brook Waimārama Sanctuary]] in [[Nelson, New Zealand|Nelson]], the largest [[Pest-exclusion fence|fenced]] sanctuary in the South Island. The last known population to have lived in the region disappeared nearly a century prior.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Gee |first=Samantha |date=8 May 2025 |title=Little spotted kiwi return to Nelson 'turning clock back a century' |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/560286/little-spotted-kiwi-return-to-nelson-turning-clock-back-a-century |access-date=2025-07-18 |website=[[RNZ]] |language=en-nz}}</ref>

In July, two little spotted kiwi were rediscovered on the mainland (outside of a fenced sanctuary) for the first time since 1978, following a tip-off from a hunter.<ref>{{Cite web |date=15 July 2025 |title=Little spotted kiwi found on New Zealand's mainland for first time in 50 years |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/news/national/567038/little-spotted-kiwi-found-on-new-zealand-s-mainland-for-first-time-in-50-years |access-date=15 July 2025 |website=RNZ |language=en-nz}}</ref> A female and male bird were found in the [[Mount Adams (New Zealand)|Adams Wilderness Area]] in the South Island's [[West Coast Region]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=16 July 2025 |title=Kiwi pukupuku/little spotted kiwi rediscovered on mainland Aotearoa after 50 years |url=https://www.stuff.co.nz/nz-news/360759060/kiwi-pukupukulittle-spotted-kiwi-rediscovered-mainland-aotearoa-after-50-years |access-date=2025-07-18 |website=www.stuff.co.nz}}</ref>

[[Category:Apteryx|little spotted kiwi]]
[[Category:Birds described in 1847|little spotted kiwi]]
[[Category:Endemic birds of New Zealand]]
[[Category:Taxa named by John Gould|little spotted kiwi]]

zh.extract:
小斑几维（学名：Apteryx owenii；英语：little spotted kiwi、little grey kiwi）是无翼属最小的一种鸟类，重不超过2公斤。它原产于新西兰，殖民者到来之前广泛分布于南北两岛，现在仅见于几个保护区和离岸小岛。


特征
栖息在密集的高大的树木组成的森林中。体长35—45 cm（14—18英寸），雄鸟重0.88—1.36公斤（1.9—3.0磅），雌鸟重1—1.95公斤（2.2—4.3磅），是最小的几维鸟。翼长0.1毫米，嘴峰长85.3毫米，喙宽度3.3毫米，喙厚度3.6毫米，跗蹠长59.6毫米，尾长0.1毫米。小斑几维在其分布区内为留鸟，其食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。
